Approximately 16 percent of married women and 20 percent of married men now report at least one extramarital partner according to the Institute for Family Studies in Charlottesville, Virginia.

Ashley Madison, a platform designed for married individuals seeking affairs, launched in 2001 in Toronto under the tagline âLife is short. Have an affair.â The site surpassed 80 million registered accounts by 2024. In 2015, hackers released the data of 32 million users. The company survived, rebranded, and continued growing. The global infidelity market now generates over $3 billion annually across platforms including Ashley Madison, Gleeden of Paris, and Victoria Milan of Oslo.

The U.S. marriage rate fell from 10.6 per 1,000 in 1970 to 6.0 per 1,000 in 2021 according to the CDC, the lowest ever recorded. The Incredibles (2004) is a landmark Pixar animated film directed by Brad Bird that reimagines the superhero genre through a family-centered lens. Set in a stylish 1960s-inspired retro-futuristic world, it explores mid-life frustration, identity, and teamwork within a family unit. Nowadays, some people donât leave directly.

They slowly change how they show up. 
Less effort. Less care. Less presence.

Until youâre the one who feels tiredâ¦ 
and chooses to walk away.

Then suddenly it becomes, 
âWhy did you leave me?â

In psychology, this is called PASSIVE EXITING 
when someone emotionally detaches first, 
but lets the other person make the final decision.

It avoids accountability, 
but still protects their image.

So you end up carrying the guilt 
for a decision they were already making with their behavior.

Pay attention to effort, not words. 
People reveal their intentions long before they leave.

Many of these abbreviations originated in early chatrooms, texting culture, and internet forums before becoming part of everyday digital language. Over time, they evolved from niche online shorthand into phrases recognized across multiple generations and platforms.

These abbreviations also reflect how communication changes alongside technology and internet culture. share.share_text: How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days (2003) production was brought to life with a highly elegant, vibrant, and stylized visual language dominated by warm golden yellows, pastel pinks, creams, and bright city lights designed to reflect the luxury and bustling life of Manhattan, aiming to bring the energetic and competitive dating dynamics of the golden age of romantic comedies to the screen. Director Donald Petrie and cinematographer John Bailey opted for an entertaining visuality, utilizing wide medium shots presenting the full depth and sociality of spaces extending from sterile advertising agency plazas to boxing matches, luxury galas, and cozy family homes, alongside sharp close-ups capturing those artificial flirtatious grimaces, glances, and sudden frustrations the characters portrayed by Matthew McConaughey and Kate Hudson put on while trying to manipulate each other to emphasize the tactical war of love built on mutual hidden agendas. This technical approach, combined with the visual harmony created by rich costume designs extending from Andie Andersonâs iconic yellow satin evening gown that went down in cinema history to Benâs sharp suits reflecting his effortless charisma, alongside the glittering, romantic silhouette of New York, gave every frame of the production both the seriousness of a modern-day romance and the most popular, cheerful, and characteristic aesthetic of early 2000s Hollywood cinema.

Lying on your resume is one of those things everyone says never do, but almost everyone has done a little bit of it.

Iâm not talking about claiming you were the CEO of a company youâve never worked for. Iâm talking about the small âenhancements.â

A few days ago I updated my resume and letâs just say I polished it. I didnât invent a fake career, but I definitely made my experience sound stronger, clearer, and a little more impressive than the plain version. ð

And guess what happened?

I got an interview. ð¥³

Thatâs when I realized something, a resume isnât a biography. Itâs marketing.

When you write âassisted with projects,â it sounds forgettable. When you write âmanaged project coordination,â suddenly it sounds like leadership. Same work, different framing.

Do I think you should lie about things you are completely unfamiliar with? No. Because the moment they ask you to talk more about it, youâre exposed.

But enhancing your experience, highlighting your strengths, and framing your work in the best possible light? Thatâs not lying. Thatâs playing the same game everyone else is playing.

Sometimes the difference between getting ignored and getting the interview is just learning how to tell your story better. And thatâs what I will try to do moving forward.

The Rubikâs Cube was invented in 1974 by Hungarian architect and professor ErnÅ Rubik, originally as a teaching tool to help students understand three-dimensional movement and geometry. At first called the âMagic Cube,â the puzzle was never intended to become a worldwide phenomenon, but after being licensed internationally in the late 1970s, it exploded into one of the best-selling toys in history. By the 1980s, Rubikâs Cubes had become a global obsession, appearing in classrooms, arcades, television shows, and competitions across the world. The cubeâs appeal came from its deceptively simple design: six colored sides and over 43 quintillion possible combinations, yet only one correct solution. For many people growing up in the 1980s and 1990s, solving a Rubikâs Cube became a symbol of patience, intelligence, and persistence.

Over time, the puzzle evolved far beyond being just a toy. Entire competitive communities formed around âspeedcubing,â where participants solve cubes in seconds using advanced memorization methods and finger movement techniques. Modern world records for the standard 3x3 cube are now under four seconds, a pace almost impossible for casual players to comprehend. The Rubikâs Cube has also remained relevant through internet culture, memes, STEM education, and social media videos where creators solve cubes blindfolded or with one hand. Decades after its release, the colorful cube continues to represent a rare combination of mathematics, design, pop culture, and nostalgia, proving that one of the most iconic puzzles ever made can still frustrate and fascinate people across multiple generations.

What makes it fascinating is the rhythm of its eruptions. Every few minutes, the volcano blasts glowing lava, ash, and smoke into the sky like a giant natural firework show. Locals living nearby have adapted to its constant activity and even consider it part of their cultural identity.

But beneath the beauty lies incredible power.
Volcanoes like Mount Yasur help scientists study Earthâs interior and understand how pressure builds beneath the planetâs crust. They also remind us that the ground beneath our feet is far from stableâitâs constantly moving and evolving.

The "6-7" trend is a viral Generation Alpha meme stemming from rapper Skrilla's song "Doot Doot (6 7)" and popularised by a viral video of a child shouting "6-7". It involves shouting or repeating the phrase, often in school, acting as a "brainrot" humor trend that serves as a shared inside joke for followers. 

Forbes
 +2
Key Aspects of the 6-7 Trend
Origin: The trend originated from American rapper Skrilla's song "Doot Doot (6 7)," which features the recurring phrase.
Viral Catalyst: It gained massive traction on TikTok and YouTube Shorts, propelled by a viral video of a young boy, known as the "67 Kid," screaming "6-7" at a basketball game, alongside clips of player LaMelo Ball (who is 6'7").
Meaning: The phrase has no inherent meaning, often described as a "joke without a punchline," "nonsense," or an "inside joke".
Usage: It is frequently shouted in classrooms, during, in, or at home, to the annoyance of teachers and parents.
Synonyms/Related Terms: It is commonly referred to as "6-7," "Six-Seven," "67 Kid," or "Doot Doot".
Cultural Context: Considered part of the broader "brainrot" phenomenonâdigital media deemed to be of poor quality but popular with Gen Alphaâand is seen as a way to express shared humor among peers.
Reaction: It has become so pervasive that some schools have banned it, while others have reported its popularity is starting to wane as it becomes too mainstream
